text: Hi, I am Dr. Shraddha Agarwal and I am going to take you through a video of dermoid cystectomy. As you can see in the video that typical scan pictures of a complex cyst with the echogenic foci most probably denoting teeth. So, with imaging we can have a fair idea that it is a dermoid cyst. You know that dermoid cyst is usually a benign cyst which could originate from a totipotential germ cell and could have content like hair, teeth, bone or endometrial tissue, thyroid etc.

The surgeon uses scissors to dissect around the dermoid cyst, taking advantage of the vasopressin-induced contraction of vessels to maintain a bloodless field. The cyst is carefully manipulated to avoid rupture and potential chemical peritoneal reaction.

In the abdominal cavity, the surgeon carefully dissects the dermoid cyst from the surrounding tissue using scissors, ensuring not to remove any ovarian tissue. The cyst is being extracted intact, with attention to preserving the ovarian tissue volume, particularly important for fertility considerations.

The surgeon carefully uses a sponge holder and forceps to dissect the dermoid cyst, ensuring not to burst it. The cyst appears as a smooth, glistening ball. Occasionally, a tooth grasper is used to hold the cyst securely to prevent slipping during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the hilum of the ovary, where a small blood vessel is visible. If necessary, the vessel can be ligated with a small stitch or using a harmonic device, although energy sources are generally avoided.

The surgeon carefully teases away and performs sharp dissection to free the dermoid cyst from surrounding tissue, ensuring it remains intact. The cyst is placed into an endo bag for removal through a ten-millimeter side port. Once secured in the bag, the cyst is ruptured to extract its contents before complete removal.
